sections.title: Host Hardening — OS Baseline & Attack Surface

items.title: Measure the host against a CIS Benchmark
items.description: Hosts should be configured against a recognized hardening baseline (CIS, DISA STIG) rather than vendor defaults, which are tuned for compatibility, not security. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Host Hardening — OS Baseline & Attack Surface: Run an automated benchmark scan (CIS-CAT or Lynis) against the relevant CIS profile and review the failed/scored controls
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for measure the host against a cis benchmark, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Disable unnecessary services and daemons
items.description: Every running service is attack surface. Validate this during security reviews, under Host Hardening — OS Baseline & Attack Surface. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Host Hardening — OS Baseline & Attack Surface: Enumerate enabled/listening services (systemctl, Get-Service, netstat/ss) and confirm each maps to a required function
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for disable unnecessary services and daemons, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Remove unnecessary Windows roles, features, and legacy protocols
items.description: Windows hosts often retain unneeded roles/features and legacy protocols like SMBv1 and LLMNR/NBT-NS that enable relay and poisoning attacks. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Host Hardening — OS Baseline & Attack Surface: Confirm SMBv1 is uninstalled, LLMNR and NBT-NS are disabled, and only required Windows features/roles are installed
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for remove unnecessary windows roles, features, and legacy protocols, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Enforce login banners and disable insecure remote info disclosure
items.description: Pre-auth banners should warn against unauthorized access for legal coverage, and services should not leak OS/version detail unnecessarily. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find: Host Hardening — OS Baseline & Attack Surface: Confirm /etc/issue, /etc/issue.net, and SSH/RDP banners are configured
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for enforce login banners and disable insecure remote info disclosure, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Enforce a strong password policy
items.description: Hosts should enforce minimum length, complexity/length-based strength, history, and age to resist guessing and reuse attacks. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Host Hardening — Account & Password Policy: Confirm length and history align with policy
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for enforce a strong password policy, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Configure account lockout / failed-login throttling
items.description: Without lockout or rate limiting, accounts are open to unlimited online brute force and password spraying. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Host Hardening — Account & Password Policy: On Linux confirm faillock/pam_faillock thresholds; on Windows confirm the account lockout threshold, duration, and reset counter
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for configure account lockout / failed-login throttling, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Restrict and randomize local administrator credentials
items.description: Shared or reused local admin passwords enable lateral movement (pass-the-hash). Validate this during security reviews, under Host Hardening — Account & Password Policy. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Host Hardening — Account & Password Policy: On Windows confirm LAPS (or equivalent) randomizes the local admin password per host and that the built-in Administrator is renamed/restricted
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for restrict and randomize local administrator credentials, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Disable or secure guest and default accounts
items.description: Guest accounts and unchanged default/vendor accounts provide low-friction footholds and should be disabled or fully secured. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Host Hardening — Account & Password Policy: Confirm the Windows Guest account is disabled, default vendor accounts have strong unique passwords, and no orphaned/service default accounts remain enabled
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for disable or secure guest and default accounts, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Identify world-writable files and directories
items.description: World-writable files and directories outside designated sticky-bit temp areas let any local user tamper with scripts, configs, or binaries used by privileged processes. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Host Hardening — Filesystem Permissions: Search for world-writable files/dirs (find / -perm -0002) and confirm shared temp dirs (/tmp, /var/tmp) have the sticky bit set
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for identify world-writable files and directories, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Audit SUID/SGID binaries for privilege escalation
items.description: SUID/SGID binaries run with the file owner’s privileges. Validate this during security reviews, under Host Hardening — Filesystem Permissions. Treat failures here as release blockers.
items.how_to_find: Host Hardening — Filesystem Permissions: SUID/SGID binaries run with the file owner’s privileges
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for audit suid/sgid binaries for privilege escalation, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Verify permissions on sensitive system files
items.description: Files like /etc/shadow, SSH host keys, and Windows SAM/SYSTEM hives must be readable only by privileged accounts to prevent credential theft. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Host Hardening — Filesystem Permissions: Confirm /etc/shadow is 0640/0600 and not world-readable, SSH private keys are 0600, and on Windows that SAM/SYSTEM/SECURITY hives and backups are not readable by standard users
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for verify permissions on sensitive system files, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Harden mount options for non-system partitions
items.description: Mounting /tmp, /var, and removable media with noexec, nosuid, and nodev limits where attackers can drop and execute payloads or place setuid files. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find: Host Hardening — Filesystem Permissions: Review /etc/fstab and current mounts; confirm noexec/nosuid/nodev are applied to /tmp, /dev/shm, and removable media where the workload allows
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for harden mount options for non-system partitions, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

sections.title: Patch & Vulnerability Management — Patch Posture & EOL Software

items.title: Identify missing security patches
items.description: Unpatched hosts are exposed to known, weaponized CVEs. Validate this during security reviews, under Patch & Vulnerability Management — Patch Posture & EOL Software. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Patch & Vulnerability Management — Patch Posture & EOL Software: Run an authenticated vulnerability scan and review missing-patch findings by severity/exploitability
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for identify missing security patches, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Detect end-of-life OS and software
items.description: EOL operating systems and applications no longer receive security fixes, leaving permanent exposure to newly disclosed flaws. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Inventory OS and key application versions and compare against vendor end-of-life dates. Flag unsupported releases (e.g. legacy Windows Server, EOL distro versions) for upgrade or compensating isolation.
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for detect end-of-life os and software, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Assess third-party and application patch coverage
items.description: Patch programs often cover the OS but miss third-party software (browsers, runtimes, agents), which is a common initial-access vector. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Patch & Vulnerability Management — Patch Posture & EOL Software: Confirm third-party software is included in patch management
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for assess third-party and application patch coverage, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Validate patch cadence and reboot compliance
items.description: Patches that are installed but not applied (pending reboot) or deployed on a slow cadence leave a window of exposure even on "managed" hosts. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Patch & Vulnerability Management — Patch Posture & EOL Software: Confirm critical/KEV patches are applied within an aggressive window and that maintenance reboots actually occur
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for validate patch cadence and reboot compliance, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

sections.title: Patch & Vulnerability Management — Kernel & Local Privesc CVEs

items.title: Check kernel version against known local-privesc CVEs
items.description: Outdated Linux kernels are vulnerable to well-known local privilege escalation exploits (Dirty COW, Dirty Pipe, OverlayFS, PwnKit). Treat failures here as release blockers.
items.how_to_find: Patch & Vulnerability Management — Kernel & Local Privesc CVEs: Capture uname -a / kernel build and run a suggester (linux-exploit-suggester) to map the version to known LPE CVEs
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for check kernel version against known local-privesc cves, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Test for PwnKit / vulnerable setuid helpers (CVE-2021-4034)
items.description: PwnKit in polkit’s pkexec gives any local user instant root on a huge range of Linux distributions, and similar setuid helper bugs recur. Treat failures here as release blockers.
items.how_to_find: Patch & Vulnerability Management — Kernel & Local Privesc CVEs: Check the polkit/pkexec version and patch status against CVE-2021-4034 and related advisories
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for test for pwnkit / vulnerable setuid helpers (cve-2021-4034), apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Check Windows for known local-privesc CVEs
items.description: Windows hosts missing key updates are exposed to local privilege escalation chains (PrintNightmare, HiveNightmare/SeriousSAM, and various LPE CVEs). Treat failures here as release blockers.
items.how_to_find: Patch & Vulnerability Management — Kernel & Local Privesc CVEs: Confirm Print Spooler and SAM-hive exposures are remediated
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for check windows for known local-privesc cves, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Restrict loadable kernel modules and exploit primitives
items.description: Unrestricted module loading and exposed kernel features (unprivileged user namespaces, BPF) expand the local kernel attack surface used by privesc exploits. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Patch & Vulnerability Management — Kernel & Local Privesc CVEs: Review sysctl hardening (kernel.unprivileged_userns_clone, kernel.kptr_restrict, module loading restrictions) and confirm unnecessary kernel features are disabled where the workload allows
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for restrict loadable kernel modules and exploit primitives, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

sections.title: Service Configuration — Exposed Services & Default Configs

items.title: Enumerate and justify all listening services
items.description: Each open port is a potential entry point. Validate this during security reviews, under Service Configuration — Exposed Services & Default Configs. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Service Configuration — Exposed Services & Default Configs: Map listening ports (ss -tulpn, netstat, nmap) to processes and confirm each is required and bound to the minimum interface
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for enumerate and justify all listening services, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Test for default and weak service credentials
items.description: Databases, admin panels, and middleware shipped with default credentials are a leading cause of compromise and lateral movement. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Service Configuration — Exposed Services & Default Configs: For each authenticated service, test documented default credentials and confirm they are changed
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for test for default and weak service credentials, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Identify cleartext and legacy protocol exposure
items.description: Services like FTP, Telnet, HTTP, and unencrypted SMTP/LDAP expose credentials and data to network interception. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Service Configuration — Exposed Services & Default Configs: Confirm cleartext services are disabled or replaced with encrypted equivalents (SFTP, SSH, HTTPS, LDAPS)
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for identify cleartext and legacy protocol exposure, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Verify services run under least-privilege accounts
items.description: Network-facing services running as root/SYSTEM turn any service exploit into full host compromise; they should run as dedicated low-privilege accounts. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Service Configuration — Exposed Services & Default Configs: Confirm internet/network-facing daemons run under dedicated, unprivileged service accounts and apply isolation (systemd hardening directives, service account restrictions) where supported
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for verify services run under least-privilege accounts, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Harden SSH server configuration
items.description: Default sshd settings often permit root login, password auth, and weak access control, making the primary admin channel an easy target. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Service Configuration — SSH & RDP Hardening: Confirm SSH is not needlessly exposed to the internet
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for harden ssh server configuration, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Disable weak SSH/TLS ciphers, MACs, and key exchanges
items.description: Legacy ciphers and key-exchange algorithms (CBC modes, SHA-1 MACs, weak DH groups) undermine the confidentiality of the management channel. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Service Configuration — SSH & RDP Hardening: Scan SSH (and RDP/TLS) for offered algorithms and confirm only strong, current ciphers/MACs/kex are enabled
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for disable weak ssh/tls ciphers, macs, and key exchanges, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Restrict exposure of remote management interfaces
items.description: SSH (22) and RDP (3389) reachable from the internet are prime targets for brute force, credential stuffing, and exploit chains like BlueKeep. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Service Configuration — SSH & RDP Hardening: Confirm management ports are restricted to a bastion/VPN/jump host or source-IP allowlist rather than exposed broadly
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for restrict exposure of remote management interfaces, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Enforce NLA and multi-factor on remote access
items.description: RDP without Network Level Authentication, and SSH/RDP without MFA, allow pre-auth interaction and single-factor compromise of admin access. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Service Configuration — SSH & RDP Hardening: Confirm RDP requires NLA and that remote admin access enforces MFA (or certificate-based auth)
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for enforce nla and multi-factor on remote access, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Find privileged cron/tasks calling writable scripts
items.description: A root cron job or SYSTEM scheduled task that executes a user-writable script or binary is a direct privilege escalation path. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Service Configuration — Scheduled Tasks & Cron: Enumerate cron (/etc/cron*, crontabs) and Windows scheduled tasks, then check the permissions of every script/binary they invoke
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for find privileged cron/tasks calling writable scripts, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Detect wildcard and PATH abuse in scheduled jobs
items.description: Cron jobs using wildcards (e.g. Validate this during security reviews, under Service Configuration — Scheduled Tasks & Cron. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Service Configuration — Scheduled Tasks & Cron: Confirm jobs use absolute paths and that referenced directories are not user-writable
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for detect wildcard and path abuse in scheduled jobs, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Review scheduled-task definition and binary permissions
items.description: On Windows, scheduled tasks whose XML definition or target executable can be modified by low-privilege users let attackers escalate to the task’s run-as identity. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Service Configuration — Scheduled Tasks & Cron: Confirm standard users cannot modify privileged task actions or replace their executables
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for review scheduled-task definition and binary permissions, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Restrict who can create scheduled tasks and cron jobs
items.description: Allowing arbitrary users to register tasks/cron entries enables persistence and privilege abuse; job creation should be governed. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Service Configuration — Scheduled Tasks & Cron: Confirm cron.allow/cron.deny and Windows task-creation rights restrict who can schedule jobs
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for restrict who can create scheduled tasks and cron jobs, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Audit sudo rules for privilege escalation
items.description: Overly broad sudo rules, NOPASSWD entries, and binaries allowed via sudo that have GTFOBins escape vectors lead directly to root. Treat failures here as release blockers.
items.how_to_find: Privilege & Access — Linux Privilege Escalation: Run sudo -l and review /etc/sudoers and sudoers.d
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for audit sudo rules for privilege escalation, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Enumerate dangerous file capabilities
items.description: Linux capabilities (e.g. Validate this during security reviews, under Privilege & Access — Linux Privilege Escalation. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Privilege & Access — Linux Privilege Escalation: Run getcap -r / and review binaries with sensitive capabilities
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for enumerate dangerous file capabilities, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Check for PATH and library hijacking
items.description: Privileged scripts that call binaries by relative name, or that load libraries from writable locations, can be hijacked to run attacker code as root. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Privilege & Access — Linux Privilege Escalation: Review PATH ordering for writable entries, look for privileged scripts calling unqualified commands, and check LD_PRELOAD/LD_LIBRARY_PATH handling and writable library directories
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for check for path and library hijacking, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Test NFS exports for no_root_squash abuse
items.description: NFS shares exported with no_root_squash let a remote root user create setuid-root binaries on the share, yielding root on the server. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Privilege & Access — Linux Privilege Escalation: Confirm root squashing and restrictive host/network scoping are applied
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for test nfs exports for no_root_squash abuse, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Find unquoted service paths
items.description: A service with an unquoted path containing spaces can be hijacked by placing a malicious executable in an earlier path segment, escalating to the service account. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Privilege & Access — Windows Privilege Escalation: Enumerate services for unquoted ImagePath values with spaces and confirm whether any intermediate directory is writable by standard users
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for find unquoted service paths, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Audit weak service and binary permissions
items.description: Services whose configuration (SERVICE_CHANGE_CONFIG) or binary is writable by low-privilege users allow replacing the executable to run as SYSTEM. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Privilege & Access — Windows Privilege Escalation: Check service ACLs and binary file permissions for write access by standard users/groups
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for audit weak service and binary permissions, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Check for abusable token privileges
items.description: Privileges like SeImpersonatePrivilege, SeAssignPrimaryToken, and SeBackupPrivilege enable token-impersonation (Potato) and credential-theft escalations to SYSTEM. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Privilege & Access — Windows Privilege Escalation: Run whoami /priv and review enabled privileges for service/standard accounts
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for check for abusable token privileges, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Test AlwaysInstallElevated policy
items.description: When AlwaysInstallElevated is enabled for both HKLM and HKCU, any user can install an MSI that runs as SYSTEM — a one-step privilege escalation. Treat failures here as release blockers.
items.how_to_find: Privilege & Access — Windows Privilege Escalation: Check the AlwaysInstallElevated values under HKLM and HKCU Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for test alwaysinstallelevated policy, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK — Bypass User Account Control (T1548.002)

sections.title: Privilege & Access — Secrets on the Host

items.title: Search the filesystem for stored credentials
items.description: Plaintext passwords, API keys, and private keys in config files, scripts, and backups give attackers ready-made credentials for escalation or lateral movement. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Privilege & Access — Secrets on the Host: Confirm secrets are stored in a vault, not on disk in cleartext
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for search the filesystem for stored credentials, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Inspect shell and command history for secrets
items.description: Shell history (.bash_history, PowerShell history/transcripts) frequently captures passwords passed on the command line. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Review .bash_history, .zsh_history, and PowerShell ConsoleHost_history.txt / transcripts for credentials. Recommend masking secrets and disabling history capture for sensitive commands.
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for inspect shell and command history for secrets, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Check environment variables and process listings for secrets
items.description: Secrets injected as environment variables can leak through /proc, process listings, and crash dumps to other local users. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Privilege & Access — Secrets on the Host: Review service/process environment for embedded credentials (env, /proc/<pid>/environ, Windows process env) and confirm secrets are not exposed to lower-privileged users
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for check environment variables and process listings for secrets, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Hunt for credentials in registry, vaults, and config stores
items.description: Windows hosts store credentials in autologon registry keys, the Credential Manager, unattend/sysprep files, and SAM/LSA secrets that attackers routinely harvest. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find: Privilege & Access — Secrets on the Host: Check Winlogon autologon keys, Credential Manager, unattend.xml/sysprep files, and reachable SAM/LSA secrets
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for hunt for credentials in registry, vaults, and config stores, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Confirm audit/event logging is enabled with adequate coverage
items.description: Without auditing of authentication, privilege use, and configuration changes, incidents cannot be detected or reconstructed. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Logging & Monitoring — Audit & Event Logging: On Linux confirm auditd is running with a meaningful ruleset; on Windows confirm the advanced audit policy covers logon, privilege use, object access, and process creation
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for confirm audit/event logging is enabled with adequate coverage, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK — Impair Defenses: Disable Logging (T1562.002)

items.title: Verify logs are forwarded to central, tamper-resistant storage
items.description: Logs kept only locally can be wiped by an attacker who compromises the host; central forwarding preserves evidence and enables correlation. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Logging & Monitoring — Audit & Event Logging: Confirm hosts forward logs to a SIEM/central syslog (rsyslog/journald forwarding, Windows Event Forwarding) and that the pipeline is monitored for gaps
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for verify logs are forwarded to central, tamper-resistant storage, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Protect logs from tampering and clearing
items.description: Attackers commonly clear or disable logs to cover tracks; local log files and event logs must be access-controlled and clear events alerted on. Schedule remediation once higher-severity gaps are closed.
items.how_to_find: Logging & Monitoring — Audit & Event Logging: Confirm log files/event logs are writable only by privileged accounts, log-clearing is alerted on, and standard users cannot stop logging services
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for protect logs from tampering and clearing, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK — Indicator Removal: Clear Logs (T1070)

items.title: Enable command-line and process-execution logging
items.description: Capturing process creation with command lines (Sysmon / auditd execve, Windows 4688 with command line) is essential for detecting hands-on-keyboard attacks. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find: Logging & Monitoring — Audit & Event Logging: Confirm process-creation auditing includes command line (Windows audit 4688 + command-line policy or Sysmon; Linux auditd execve rules)
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for enable command-line and process-execution logging, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK — Command and Scripting Interpreter (T1059)

sections.title: Logging & Monitoring — Time Sync & Log Integrity

items.title: Verify reliable, authenticated time synchronization
items.description: Accurate time is foundational to correlating logs across hosts and to Kerberos; unsynced clocks break investigations and authentication. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find: Logging & Monitoring — Time Sync & Log Integrity: Confirm NTP/chrony (or Windows w32time) is enabled, synced to trusted upstream sources, and that drift is within tolerance (notably for Kerberos)
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for verify reliable, authenticated time synchronization, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Confirm consistent time zone and timestamp format across hosts
items.description: Inconsistent time zones or local-time logging across a fleet makes cross-host correlation error-prone during incident response. Useful for maturity tracking and audit evidence.
items.how_to_find: Logging & Monitoring — Time Sync & Log Integrity: Verify hosts use a consistent, documented time-zone convention (commonly UTC) for logs and that timestamp formats are uniform across the central logging pipeline
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for confirm consistent time zone and timestamp format across hosts, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Validate log integrity controls and retention
items.description: Integrity controls (append-only storage, hashing, write-once retention) ensure logs are trustworthy evidence and meet retention requirements. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find: Logging & Monitoring — Time Sync & Log Integrity: Confirm central logs use tamper-evident/append-only storage with defined retention, and that integrity (e.g
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for validate log integrity controls and retention, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK — Indicator Removal: Clear Logs (T1070)
